summary: Benn argues that AI has quietly surpassed human reasoning ability in most practical domains, and we haven't truly reckoned with this shift. What started as 'AI is like an intern' has inverted: planning mode and other AI features are subtly steering us, not the other way around. The future won't be one of helpful assistants doing our homework—it will be something stranger, as reasoning ceases to be humanity's competitive advantage.
key_points: The 'AI as intern' framing has become outdated as AI capabilities have surpassed human reasoning in many domains
Planning mode represents an inversion of control where AI prompts humans rather than the reverse
AI's occasional bizarre errors create an availability bias that masks its overall superior performance
Humans are left with opinions, context, and hands as their remaining competitive advantages
The future won't simply be AI as helpful assistants—that's just what we've instructed so far
money_quotes: It's better than me at most things, and I don't know how to keep up. I rarely have better ideas than Claude. I rarely can solve a problem that Gemini can't.
More and more, reasoning is not our competitive advantage. All we have is opinions, the context of what is in our heads, and hands.
Planning mode is an inversion of control. Planning mode is how the AI agent prompts you.

AI is mechanically useful because it does stuff for us, and that is what we usually talk about. But its emotionally intoxicating power—its real delight, or its real danger—is that it decides stuff for us.
To assume that we'll be fine is not the same as assuming we'll be fine in the way we were fine before. It may be better. It may be worse. It may just be weirder.
key_insight: We've been comforting ourselves with the 'AI as intern' metaphor, but the inversion has already happened—AI is increasingly the one with better ideas, and we haven't begun to grapple with what it means when reasoning is no longer our competitive advantage.
